chunks.title: Introduction and Statement of Objects and Reasons
chunks.chapter: Introduction
chunks.text:
INTRODUCTION

For rural development, the major focus of planning had been productive absorption of under-employed and surplus labour force of the rural sector. In order to provide direct supplementary wage-employment to the rural poor through public works, many programmes were initiated by the Government of India, namely, National Rural Employment Programme (NREP), Rural Landless Employment Guarantee Programme (RLEGP) and Jawahar Rozgar Yojana (JRY). Currently, Sampoorna Gramin Rozgar Yojana (SGRY) is being implemented all over the country with the objective to provide supplementary wage employment in rural areas, create durable rural infrastructure and to ensure food security.

The scale of employment generation under SGRY in 2002-03 and 2003-04 was barely adequate to provide on an average 20 days of employment to each Below Poverty Line (BPL) household in rural areas. There is no guarantee that employment will be available to rural households on demand as SGRY is an allocation-based programme. The situation of unemployment has been compounded by the absence of any social security mechanism.

STATEMENT OF OBJECTS AND REASONS

The proposed legislation constitutes a pioneering endeavour to secure wage employment for poor households in rural areas as a guaranteed entitlement. It takes into account the experience gained under the Employment Guarantee Scheme in Maharashtra. The salient features of the proposed legislation are: (i) providing at least 100 days of guaranteed wage employment to every poor household whose adult members volunteer to do unskilled manual work; (ii) State Governments shall prepare a scheme within one year; (iii) employment at a wage rate specified by the Central Government, not less than Rs. 60 per day; (iv) if employment is not provided within 15 days, unemployment allowance shall be paid; (v) Central and State Employment Guarantee Councils shall be constituted; (vi) a National Employment Guarantee Fund shall be established.

chunks.title: Chapter I – Preliminary (Sections 1–2): Short Title, Extent and Definitions
chunks.chapter: Chapter I – Preliminary
chunks.text:
CHAPTER I – PRELIMINARY

Section 1 – Short title, extent and commencement

This Act may be called the Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act, 2005. It extends to the whole of India. It shall come into force on such date as the Central Government may, by notification in the Official Gazette, appoint; different dates may be appointed for different States or different areas in a State. The Act shall be applicable to the whole territory within a period of five years from the date of its enacthent.

Section 2 – Definitions

In this Act, unless the context otherwise requires:
(a) 'adult' means a person who has completed his eighteenth year of age.
(b) 'applicant' means the head of a household or any other adult member who has applied for employment under the Scheme.
(c) 'Block' means a community development area within a district comprising a group of Gram Panchayats.
(d) 'Central Council' means the Central Employment Guarantee Council constituted under sub-section (1) of section 10.
(e) 'District Programme Coordinator' means an officer of the State Government designated under sub-section (1) of section 14 for implementation of the Scheme in a district.
(f) 'household' means the members of a family related to each other by blood, marriage or adoption and normally residing together and sharing meals or holding a common ration card.
(g) 'implementing agency' includes any departhent of the Central or State Government, a Zila Parishad, Panchayat at intermediate level, Gram Panchayat or any local authority or Government undertaking or non-governmental organisation authorised by the Central or State Government to undertake implementation of any work under a Scheme.
(h) 'minimum wage' means the minimum wage fixed by the State Government under section 3 of the Minimum Wages Act, 1948 for agricultural labourers.
(i) 'National Fund' means the National Employment Guarantee Fund established under sub-section (1) of section 20.
(j) 'notification' means a notification published in the Official Gazette.
(k) 'preferred work' means any work which is taken up for implementation on a priority basis under a Scheme.
(l) 'prescribed' means prescribed by rules made under this Act.
(m) 'Programme Officer' means an officer appointed under sub-section (1) of section 15 for implementing the Scheme.
(n) 'project' means any work taken up under a Scheme for the purpose of providing employment to the applicants.
(o) 'rural area' means any area in a State except those areas covered by any urban local body or a Cantonment Board.
(p) 'Scheme' means a Scheme notified by the State Government under sub-section (1) of section 4.
(q) 'State Council' means the State Employment Guarantee Council constituted under sub-section (1) of section 12.
(r) 'unskilled manual work' means any physical work which any adult person is capable of doing without any skill or special training.
(s) 'wage rate' means the wage rate referred to in section 6.

chunks.title: Chapter II – Guarantee of Rural Employment (Section 3)
chunks.chapter: Chapter II – Guarantee of Employment in Rural Areas
chunks.text:
CHAPTER II – GUARANTEE OF EMPLOYMENT IN RURAL AREAS

Section 3 – Guarantee of rural employment to households

(1) Save as otherwise provided, the State Government shall, in such rural area in the State as may be notified by the Central Government, provide to every household whose adult members volunteer to do unskilled manual work not less than one hundred days of such work in a financial year in accordance with the Scheme made under this Act.

(2) Every person who has done the work given to him under the Scheme shall be entitled to receive wages at the wage rate for each day of work.

(3) Save as otherwise provided in this Act, the disbursement of daily wages shall be made on a weekly basis or in any case not later than a fortnight after the date on which such work was done.

(4) The Central Government or the State Government may, within the limits of its economic capacity and development, make provisions for securing work to every adult member of a household under a Scheme for any period beyond the period guaranteed under sub-section (1).

chunks.title: Chapter III – Employment Guarantee Schemes and Conditions (Sections 4–5)
chunks.chapter: Chapter III – Employment Guarantee Schemes and Unemployment Allowance
chunks.text:
CHAPTER III – EMPLOYMENT GUARANTEE SCHEMES AND UNEMPLOYMENT ALLOWANCE

Section 4 – Employment Guarantee Schemes for rural areas

(1) For the purposes of giving effect to the provisions of section 3, every State Government shall, within one year from the date of commencement of this Act, by notification, make a Scheme, for providing not less than one hundred days of guaranteed employment in a financial year to every household in the rural areas covered under the Scheme and whose adult members, by application, volunteer to do unskilled manual work.

Provided that until any such Scheme is notified by the State Government, the Annual Action Plan or Perspective Plan for the Sampoorna Grameen Rozgar Yojana (SGRY) or the National Food for Work Programme (NFFWP) whichever is in force in the concerned area immediately before such notification shall be deemed to be the action plan for the Scheme.

(2) The State Government shall publish a summary of the Scheme made by it in at least two local newspapers, one of which shall be in a vernacular language circulating in the area to which such Scheme shall apply.

(3) The Scheme made under sub-section (1) shall provide for the minimum features specified in Schedule I.

Section 5 – Conditions for providing guaranteed employment

(1) The State Government may, without prejudice to the conditions specified in Schedule II, specify in the Scheme the conditions for providing guaranteed employment under this Act.

(2) The persons employed under any Scheme made under this Act shall be entitled to such facilities not less than the minimum facilities specified in Schedule II.

chunks.title: Chapter III – Wage Rate and Unemployment Allowance (Sections 6–9)
chunks.chapter: Chapter III – Employment Guarantee Schemes and Unemployment Allowance
chunks.text:
Section 6 – Wage rate

(1) Notwithstanding anything contained in the Minimum Wages Act, 1948, the Central Government may, by notification, specify the wage rate for the purposes of this Act. Different rates of wages may be specified for different areas. The wage rate specified shall not be at a rate less than sixty rupees per day.

(2) Until such time as a wage rate is fixed by the Central Government in respect of any area in a State, the minimum wage fixed by the State Government under section 3 of the Minimum Wages Act, 1948 for agricultural labourers shall be considered as the wage rate applicable to that area.

Section 7 – Payment of unemployment allowance

(1) If an applicant for employment under the Scheme is not provided such employment within fifteen days of receipt of his application seeking employment or from the date on which the employment has been sought in the case of an advance application, whichever is later, he shall be entitled to a daily unemployment allowance.

(2) The unemployment allowance payable shall not be less than one-fourth of the wage rate for the first thirty days during the financial year and not less than one-half of the wage rate for the remaining period of the financial year.

(3) The liability of the State Government to pay unemployment allowance to a household during any financial year shall cease as soon as: (a) the applicant is directed by the Gram Panchayat or Programme Officer to report for work; (b) the period for which employment is sought comes to an end and no member of the household had turned up for employment; (c) the adult members of the household have received at least one hundred days of work within the financial year; or (d) the household has earned as much from wages and unemployment allowance taken together which is equal to the wages for one hundred days of work during the financial year.

(4) The unemployment allowance payable to the household jointly shall be sanctioned and disbursed by the Programme Officer or such local authority as the State Government may authorise.

(5) Every payment of unemployment allowance shall be made or offered not later than fifteen days from the date on which it became due for payment.

Section 8 – Non-disbursement of unemployment allowance in certain circumstances

If the Programme Officer is not in a position to disburse the unemployment allowance in time or at all for any reason beyond his control, he shall report the matter to the District Programme Coordinator and announce such reasons in a notice to be displayed on his notice board and the notice board of the Gram Panchayat. Every case of non-payment or delayed payment shall be reported in the annual report submitted by the District Programme Coordinator to the State Government.

Section 9 – Disentitlement to receive unemployment allowance

An applicant who: (a) does not accept the employment provided to his household under a Scheme; (b) does not report for work within fifteen days of being notified by the Programme Officer; or (c) continuously remains absent from work without permission for more than one week or remains absent for a total period of more than one week in any month, shall not be eligible to claim the unemployment allowance for a period of three months but shall be eligible to seek employment under the Scheme at any time.

chunks.title: Chapter IV – Central Employment Guarantee Council (Sections 10–11)
chunks.chapter: Chapter IV – Implementing and Monitoring Authorities
chunks.text:
CHAPTER IV – IMPLEMENTING AND MONITORING AUTHORITIES

Section 10 – Central Employment Guarantee Council

(1) There shall be constituted a Council to be called the Central Employment Guarantee Council to discharge the functions and perform the duties assigned to it under this Act. The headquarters of the Central Council shall be at Delhi.

(3) The Central Council shall consist of the following members appointed by the Central Government: (a) a Chairperson; (b) not more than such number of representatives of the Central Ministries including the Planning Commission not below the rank of Joint Secretary as may be determined by the Central Government; (c) not more than such number of representatives of the State Governments as may be determined; (d) not more than fifteen non-official members representing Panchayati Raj Institutions, organisations of workers and disadvantaged groups — not less than one-third shall be women, and not less than one-third shall belong to Scheduled Castes, Scheduled Tribes, Other Backward Classes and Minorities; (e) such number of representatives of the States as may be determined; (f) a Member-Secretary not below the rank of Joint Secretary.

Section 11 – Functions and duties of Central Council

(1) The Central Council shall: (a) establish a central evaluation and monitoring system; (b) advise the Central Government on all matters concerning the implementation of this Act; (c) review the monitoring and redressal mechanism from time to time and recommend improvements; (d) promote the widest possible dissemination of information about the Schemes; (e) monitor the implementation of this Act; (f) prepare annual reports to be laid before Parliament; (g) any other duty or function assigned to it by the Central Government.

(2) The Central Council shall have the power to undertake evaluation of the various Schemes made under this Act and collect statistics pertaining to the rural economy and the implementation of the Schemes.

chunks.title: Chapter IV – State Employment Guarantee Council (Section 12)
chunks.chapter: Chapter IV – Implementing and Monitoring Authorities
chunks.text:
Section 12 – State Employment Guarantee Council

(1) For the purposes of regular monitoring and reviewing the implementation of this Act at the State level, every State Government shall constitute a State Council to be known as the [State Name State Employment Guarantee Council with a Chairperson and such number of official members as may be determined by the State Government and not more than fifteen non-official members nominated from Panchayati Raj institutions, organisations of workers and disadvantaged groups. Not less than one-third of the non-official members shall be women; not less than one-third shall belong to Scheduled Castes, Scheduled Tribes, Other Backward Classes and Minorities.

(3) The duties and functions of the State Council shall include: (a) advising the State Government on all matters concerning the Scheme and its implementation; (b) determining the preferred works; (c) reviewing the monitoring and redressal mechanisms and recommending improvements; (d) promoting the widest possible dissemination of information about this Act and the Schemes; (e) monitoring the implementation of this Act and the Schemes in the State and coordinating with the Central Council; (f) preparing the annual report to be laid before the State Legislature; (g) any other duty or function assigned to it by the Central Council or the State Government.

chunks.title: Chapter IV – Principal Authorities for Planning (Section 13)
chunks.chapter: Chapter IV – Implementing and Monitoring Authorities
chunks.text:
Section 13 – Principal authorities for planning and implementation of Schemes

(1) The Panchayats at district, intermediate and village levels shall be the principal authorities for planning and implementation of the Schemes made under this Act.

(2) The functions of the Panchayats at the district level shall be: (a) to finalise and approve blockwise shelf of projects to be taken up under a programme under the Scheme; (b) to supervise and monitor the projects taken up at the Block level and district level; (c) to carry out such other functions as may be assigned to it by the State Council.

(3) The functions of the Panchayat at intermediate level shall be: (a) to approve the Block level Plan for forwarding it to the district Panchayat for final approval; (b) to supervise and monitor the projects taken up at the Gram Panchayat and Block level; (c) to carry out such other functions as may be assigned to it by the State Council.

(4) The District Programme Coordinator shall assist the Panchayat at the district level in discharging its functions under this Act.

chunks.title: Chapter IV – District Programme Coordinator (Section 14)
chunks.chapter: Chapter IV – Implementing and Monitoring Authorities
chunks.text:
Section 14 – District Programme Coordinator

(1) The Chief Executive Officer of the District Panchayat or the Collector of the district or any other district level officer of appropriate rank as the State Government may decide shall be designated as the District Programme Coordinator for the implementation of the Scheme in the district.

(2) The District Programme Coordinator shall be responsible for the implementation of the Scheme in the district in accordance with the provisions of this Act.

(3) The functions of the District Programme Coordinator shall be: (a) to assist the district Panchayat in discharging its functions; (b) to consolidate the plans prepared by the Blocks and project proposals for inclusion in the shelf of projects to be approved by the Panchayat at district level; (c) to accord necessary sanction and administrative clearance; (d) to coordinate with Programme Officers and implementing agencies to ensure that applicants are provided employment as per their entitlements; (e) to review, monitor and supervise the performance of Programme Officers; (f) to conduct periodic inspection of works in progress; (g) to redress the grievances of applicants.

(6) The District Programme Coordinator shall prepare in the month of December every year a labour budget for the next financial year containing details of anticipated demand for unskilled manual work in the district and the plan for engagement of labourers in works covered under the Scheme, and submit it to the district panchayat.

chunks.title: Chapter IV – Programme Officer (Section 15)
chunks.chapter: Chapter IV – Implementing and Monitoring Authorities
chunks.text:
Section 15 – Programme Officer

(1) At every Panchayat at intermediate level, the State Government shall appoint a person who is not below the rank of Block Development Officer as Programme Officer.

(3) The Programme Officer shall be responsible for matching the demand for employment with the employment opportunities arising from projects in the area under his jurisdiction.

(4) The Programme Officer shall prepare a plan for the Block under his jurisdiction by consolidating the project proposals prepared by the Gram Panchayats.

(5) The functions of the Programme Officer shall include: (a) monitoring of projects taken up by the Gram Panchayats and other implementing agencies within the Block; (b) sanctioning and ensuring payment of unemployment allowance to eligible households; (c) ensuring prompt and fair payment of wages to all labourers employed under a programme within the Block; (d) ensuring that regular social audits of all works within the jurisdiction of the Gram Panchayat are carried out by the Gram Sabha and that prompt action is taken on objections raised in the social audit; (e) dealing promptly with all complaints in connection with the implementation of the Scheme; (f) any other work assigned by the District Programme Coordinator or the State Government.

(6) The Programme Officers shall function under the direction, control and superintendence of the District Programme Coordinator.

(7) The State Government may direct that all or any of the functions of a Programme Officer shall be discharged by the Gram Panchayat or a local authority.

chunks.title: Chapter IV – Gram Panchayat Responsibilities and Social Audit (Sections 16–17)
chunks.chapter: Chapter IV – Implementing and Monitoring Authorities
chunks.text:
Section 16 – Responsibilities of the Gram Panchayats

(1) The Gram Panchayat shall be responsible for identification of the projects in the Gram Panchayat area to be taken up under a Scheme as per the recommendations of the Gram Sabha and the Ward Sabhas and for executing and supervising such works.

(3) Every Gram Panchayat shall, after considering the recommendations of the Gram Sabha and the Ward Sabhas, prepare a development plan and maintain a shelf of possible works to be taken up under the Scheme as and when demand for work arises.

(5) The Programme Officer shall allot at least fifty per cent of the works in terms of cost under a Scheme to be implemented through the Gram Panchayats.

(7) The Gram Panchayat shall allocate employment opportunities among the applicants and ask them to report for work.

Section 17 – Social audit of work by Gram Sabha

(1) The Gram Sabha shall monitor the execution of works within the Gram Panchayat.

(2) The Gram Sabha shall conduct regular social audits of all the projects under the Scheme taken up within the Gram Panchayat.

(3) The Gram Panchayat shall make available all relevant documents including the muster rolls, bills, vouchers, measurement books, copies of sanction orders and other connected books of account and papers to the Gram Sabha for the purpose of conducting the social audit.

Section 18 – Responsibilities of State Government in implementing Scheme

The State Government shall make available to the District Programme Coordinator and the Programme Officers necessary staff and technical support as may be necessary for the effective implementation of the Scheme.

Section 19 – Grievance redressal mechanism

The State Government shall, by rules, determine appropriate grievance redressal mechanisms at the Block level and the district level for dealing with any complaint by any person in respect of implementation of the Scheme and lay down the procedure for disposal of such complaints.

chunks.title: Chapter V – National and State Employment Guarantee Funds (Sections 20–22)
chunks.chapter: Chapter V – Establishment of National and State Employment Guarantee Funds and Audit
chunks.text:
CHAPTER V – ESTABLISHMENT OF NATIONAL AND STATE EMPLOYMENT GUARANTEE FUNDS AND AUDIT

Section 20 – National Employment Guarantee Fund

(1) The Central Government shall, by notification, establish a fund to be called the National Employment Guarantee Fund for the purposes of this Act.

(2) The Central Government may, after due appropriation made by Parliament, credit by way of grants or loans such sums of money as it may consider necessary to the National Fund.

(3) The amount standing to the credit of the National Fund shall be utilised in such manner and subject to such conditions and limitations as may be prescribed by the Central Government.

Section 21 – State Employment Guarantee Fund

(1) The State Government may, by notification, establish a fund to be called the State Employment Guarantee Fund for the purposes of implementation of the Scheme.

(2) The amount standing to the credit of the State Fund shall be expended for the purposes of implementation of this Act and the Schemes made thereunder and for meeting administrative expenses in connection with the implementation of this Act.

Section 22 – Funding pattern

(1) The Central Government shall meet the cost of: (a) the amount required for payment of wages for unskilled manual work; (b) up to three-fourths of the material cost of the Scheme including payment of wages to skilled and semi-skilled workers; (c) such percentage of the total cost towards administrative expenses as may be determined by the Central Government.

(2) The State Government shall meet the cost of: (a) the cost of unemployment allowance payable under the Scheme; (b) one-fourth of the material cost of the Scheme including payment of wages to skilled and semi-skilled workers; (c) the administrative expenses of the State Council.

chunks.title: Chapter V – Transparency, Accountability and Audit (Sections 23–24)
chunks.chapter: Chapter V – Establishment of National and State Employment Guarantee Funds and Audit
chunks.text:
Section 23 – Transparency and accountability

(1) The District Programme Coordinator and all implementing agencies in the District shall be responsible for the proper utilisation and management of the funds placed at their disposal for the purpose of implementing a Scheme.

(3) The State Government may, by rules, determine the arrangements to be made for the proper execution of Schemes and programmes and to ensure transparency and accountability at all levels in the implementation of the Schemes.

(4) All payments of wages in cash and unemployment allowances shall be made directly to the person concerned and in the presence of independent persons of the community on pre-announced dates.

(5) If any dispute or complaint arises concerning the implementation of a Scheme by the Gram Panchayat, the matter shall be referred to the Programme Officer.

(6) The Programme Officer shall enter every complaint in a complaint register and shall dispose of disputes and complaints within seven days of receipt.

Section 24 – Audit of accounts

(1) The Central Government may, in consultation with the Comptroller and Auditor General of India, prescribe appropriate arrangements for audits of the accounts of the Schemes at all levels.

(2) The accounts of the Scheme shall be maintained in such form and in such manner as may be prescribed by the State Government.

chunks.title: Chapter VI – Miscellaneous Provisions (Sections 25–34)
chunks.chapter: Chapter VI – Miscellaneous
chunks.text:
CHAPTER VI – MISCELLANEOUS

Section 25 – Penalty for non-compliance
Whoever contravenes the provisions of this Act shall on conviction be liable to a fine which may extend to one thousand rupees.

Section 26 – Power to delegate
The Central Government may direct that the powers exercisable by it (excluding the power to make rules) may be exercisable also by the State Government or such officer subordinate to the Central or State Government.

Section 27 – Power of Central Government to give directions
(1) The Central Government may give such directions as it may consider necessary to the State Government for the effective implementation of the provisions of this Act.
(2) On receipt of any complaint regarding improper utilisation of funds, the Central Government may cause an investigation and if necessary, order stoppage of release of funds to the Scheme and institute appropriate remedial measures.

Section 28 – Act to have overriding effect
The provisions of this Act or the Schemes made thereunder shall have effect notwithstanding anything inconsistent therewith contained in any other law for the time being in force. However, where a State enacthent exists to provide employment guarantee for unskilled manual work to rural households consistent with the provisions of this Act, under which the entitlement of the households is not less than and the conditions of employment are not inferior to what is guaranteed under this Act, the State Government shall have the option of implementing its own enacthent.

Section 29 – Power to amend Schedules
The Central Government may, by notification, amend Schedule I or Schedule II if it is necessary or expedient to do so.

Section 30 – Protection of action taken in good faith
No suit, prosecution or other legal proceedings shall lie against the District Programme Coordinator, Programme Officer or any other public servant in respect of anything which is in good faith done or intended to be done under this Act.

Section 31 – Power of Central Government to make rules
The Central Government may, by notification, make rules to carry out the provisions of this Act, including rules relating to: the composition of the Central Council; the utilisation of the National Fund; the funding pattern.

Section 32 – Power of State Government to make rules
The State Government may make rules consistent with this Act and the rules made by the Central Government, including rules relating to: eligibility for unemployment allowance; grievance redressal mechanisms; maintenance of books of accounts; arrangements for proper execution of Schemes.

Section 33 – Laying of rules and Schemes
Every rule made by the Central Government shall be laid before each House of Parliament. Every rule or Scheme made by the State Government shall be laid before each House of the State Legislature.

Section 34 – Power to remove difficulties
If any difficulty arises in giving effect to the provisions of this Act, the Central Government may, by order published in the Official Gazette, make such provisions as appear to it to be necessary or expedient for removing the difficulty. No order shall be made under this section after the expiry of three years from the commencement of this Act.

chunks.title: Schedule I – Minimum Features of REGS: Objectives and Work Categories A & B
chunks.chapter: Schedule I

chunks.title: Schedule I – Work Categories C & D and Planning Provisions (Paragraphs 6–14)
chunks.chapter: Schedule I

chunks.title: Schedule I – Muster Rolls, Wages, No-Contractor Rule and Transparency (Paragraphs 15–25)
chunks.chapter: Schedule I

chunks.title: Schedule I – Social Audit, Grievance Redressal and Ombudsperson (Paragraphs 25c–35)
chunks.chapter: Schedule I

chunks.title: Schedule II – Job Cards: Registration and Issuance (Paragraphs 1–5)
chunks.chapter: Schedule II – Conditions for Guaranteed Rural Employment and Minimum Entitlements
chunks.text:
SCHEDULE II – CONDITIONS FOR GUARANTEED RURAL EMPLOYMENT UNDER A SCHEME AND MINIMUM ENTITLEMENTS OF LABOURERS

JOB CARDS

1. The adult member of every household residing in any rural area and willing to do unskilled manual work may submit the names, age and address of the household to the Gram Panchayat for registration and issuance of a job card. If the job seeker is a single woman, disabled person, aged person, released bonded labour or belonging to Particularly Vulnerable Tribal Group, they must be given a special job card of a distinct colour.

2. It shall be the duty of the Gram Panchayat to issue a job card within fifteen days from the date of receipt of such application. The job card shall contain a unique job card number, details of the registered adult members of the household, their photos, Bank or Post office account number, insurance policy number and the Aadhaar numbers if any.

3. The job card issued shall be valid for at least five years after which it may be renewed after due verification.

4. No job card can be cancelled except where it is found to be a duplicate, or if the entire household has permanently migrated outside the Gram Panchayat.

5. The State Government shall make arrangements for updating the following details in the job card regularly: (i) number of days for which work was demanded; (ii) number of days of work allocated; (iii) description of work allocated with muster roll number; (iv) measurement details; (v) unemployment allowance paid if any; (vi) dates and number of days worked; (vii) date-wise amount of wages paid; (viii) delay compensation paid if any.

chunks.title: Schedule II – Demand for Work and Allocation of Work (Paragraphs 6–21)
chunks.chapter: Schedule II – Conditions for Guaranteed Rural Employment and Minimum Entitlements
chunks.text:
DEMAND FOR WORK

6. Every adult member of a registered household whose name appears in the job card shall be entitled to apply for unskilled manual work under the Scheme; and every such application shall be compulsorily registered, and a receipt issued with the date, which shall be entered in the computer system.

8. Application for work can be oral or written and made to the Ward member, Gram Panchayat, Programme Officer, or through telephone, mobile, Interactive Voice Response System, call centre, website, kiosk, or any other means authorised by the State Government.

10. There shall be no limit on the number of days of employment for which a person may apply, subject to the aggregate entitlement of the household.

11. Normally, applications for work must be for at least fourteen days of continuous work. For sanitation-related works, application shall be for at least six days of continuous work.

ALLOCATION OF WORK

14. The Gram Panchayat and Programme Officer shall ensure that every applicant shall be provided unskilled manual work within fifteen days of receipt of an application or the date from which he seeks work in case of advance application, whichever is later.

15. Priority shall be given to women in such a way that at least one-third of the beneficiaries shall be women who have registered and requested for work.

18. As far as possible, employment shall be provided within a radius of five kilometres of the village where the applicant resides at the time of applying.

19. A new work under the Scheme can be commenced if at least ten labourers become available for work (not applicable for hilly areas and afforestation works).

20. In case employment is provided outside the five kilometre radius, it shall be provided within the Block, and the labourers shall be paid ten percent of the wage rate as extra wages to meet additional transportation and living expenses.

21. A period of employment shall be for at least fourteen days continuously with not more than six days in a week.

chunks.title: Schedule II – Worksite Management and Welfare Provisions (Paragraphs 22–28)
chunks.chapter: Schedule II – Conditions for Guaranteed Rural Employment and Minimum Entitlements
chunks.text:
WORKSITE MANAGEMENT

22. For transparency at the worksite, the following shall be ensured: (i) a project initiation meeting in which provisions of the work shall be explained to workers; (ii) a copy of the sanction work order shall be available for public inspection at the worksite; (iii) measurement record of each work and details of workers shall be available for public inspection; (iv) a Citizens' Information Board shall be put up at every worksite and updated regularly.

23. The facilities of safe drinking water, shade for children and periods of rest, first aid box with adequate material for emergency treathent shall be provided at the work site.

24. In case the number of children below the age of five years accompanying women workers at any site is five or more, provisions shall be made to depute one of such women workers to look after such children. The person so deputed shall be paid the wage rate.

WELFARE

25. If any personal injury is caused to any person employed under the Scheme by any accident arising out of and in the course of his employment, he shall be entitled to such medical treathent as required, free of cost.

26. Where hospitalisation of the injured worker is necessary, the State Government shall arrange for such hospitalisation including accommodation, treathent, medicines and payment of daily allowance which is not less than half of the wage rate.

27. If a person employed under the Scheme meets with death or becomes permanently disabled by accident arising out of and in the course of employment, he or his legal heirs shall be paid an ex gratia as per entitlements under the Aam Aadmi Bima Yojana or as may be notified by the Central Government.

28. If any personal injury is caused by accident to a child accompanying a person who is employed under the Scheme, such person shall be entitled to medical treathent free of cost; and in case of death or disablement of the child, ex gratia shall be paid to the legal guardians as determined by the State Government.

chunks.title: Schedule II – Wage Payment, Record Maintenance and Grievance Redressal (Paragraphs 29–35)
chunks.chapter: Schedule II – Conditions for Guaranteed Rural Employment and Minimum Entitlements
chunks.text:
WAGE PAYMENT

29. (1) In case the payment of wages is not made within fifteen days from the date of closure of the muster roll, the wage seekers shall be entitled to receive compensation for the delay at the rate of 0.05% of the unpaid wages per day of delay beyond the sixteenth day of closure of muster roll.

(b) For accountability in payment of wages, States shall divide the process into stages: (i) measurement of work; (ii) computerising the muster rolls; (iii) computerising the measurements; (iv) generation of wage lists; (v) uploading Fund Transfer Orders (FTOs), and specify stage-wise maximum time limits.

(d) The State Government shall pay the compensation upfront after due verification and recover the compensation amount from the functionaries or agencies responsible for the delay in payment.

30. The payment of wages shall, unless exempted by the Central Government, be made through the individual savings accounts of the workers in the relevant Banks or Post Offices.

31. In case of employment provided under the Scheme, there shall be no discrimination solely on the ground of gender and the provisions of the Equal Remuneration Act, 1976 shall be complied with.

RECORD MAINTENANCE AND GRIEVANCE REDRESSAL

34. (1) Notwithstanding anything in this Schedule, in the event of any national calamity such as flood, cyclone, tsunami or earthquake resulting in mass dislocation of rural population, the adult members of rural households of areas so affected may seek registration and get job cards issued by the Gram Panchayat or Programme Officer of the area of temporary relocation.

35. Every worker shall have an opportunity of being heard and register any grievance either orally or in writing, at all implementation levels for disposal as per the provisions of the Grievance Redressal Mechanism under the Scheme.

chunks.title: National Rural Employment Guarantee (Central Council) Rules, 2006 – Composition and Terms (Rules 1–5)
chunks.chapter: Central Council Rules 2006

chunks.title: Central Council Rules 2006 – Meetings, Functions, Executive Committee and Funds (Rules 6–12)
chunks.chapter: Central Council Rules 2006
chunks.text:
Rule 6 – Meetings of the Central Council and its quorum:
The Central Council shall meet at least two times in a year. Not less than one-third of the members shall constitute the quorum. Six months shall not intervene between two consecutive meetings.

Rule 7 – Procedure of the meetings:
The Member-Secretary shall give at least fourteen clear days notice for a meeting. Every question shall be decided upon by a majority of members present and voting. In case of equality of votes, the Chairperson shall have a casting vote. Minutes shall be circulated within thirty days of a meeting.

Rule 9 – Executive Committee:
The Central Council shall constitute an Executive Committee consisting of: the Secretary, Departhent of Rural Development (President); the Financial Advisor, Ministry of Rural Development; representative of Ministry of Panchayati Raj and two Secretaries of State Governments; four non-official members of the Central Council.

Rule 10 – Functions of the Executive Committee:
The Executive Committee shall: take steps to give effect to decisions of the Central Council; manage administrative and financial affairs; sanction expenditure; appoint expert groups for technical support. It shall meet at least once in three months.

Rule 12 – Funds of the Central Council:
The Central Council shall meet its expenses from the annual grants released to it by the Ministry of Rural Development out of the National Employment Guarantee Fund. The funds shall be operated through a scheduled bank as approved by the Central Council.

chunks.title: National Employment Guarantee Fund Rules, 2006
chunks.chapter: Fund Rules 2006
chunks.text:
THE NATIONAL EMPLOYMENT GUARANTEE FUND RULES, 2006
(Came into force on 3-1-2007)

Rule 3 – Account of the National Fund:
The National Fund shall be maintained by the Central Government in a Public Account.

Rule 4 – Usage of the National Fund:
The National Fund shall be used by the Central Government to meet the Central Government share of expenditure on implementation of the Act including grants to the State Governments and Union territories and the expenses of the Central Employment Guarantee Council.

Rule 5 – Release of grants from the Fund to the State Governments and Union Territory Administrations:
(1) Before the beginning of each financial year on or before 31st January, all Secretaries of the State Governments concerned with implementation of the Act shall present their annual work plan and labour budget to the Ministry of Rural Development.
(3) The Ministry of Rural Development may examine the proposals and review the performance of the States and estimate the amount to be released to the State Governments from the National Fund.
(6) The sanctioned amount may be released directly from the National Employment Guarantee Fund to the State Employment Guarantee Fund.
(8) The State Employment Guarantee Fund shall be maintained in a separate bank account which shall be non-lapsable.
(10) The sanctioned amount may also be released directly from the National Fund to a separate bank account at the district level for implementation, which shall be non-lapsable.

Rule 6 – Release of grants from the National Fund to the Central Employment Guarantee Council:
The Council shall be given a grant every year with an initial corpus of five crores of rupees. The grants shall be given to the Council after audit report for the previous year and the utilisation certificate for at least sixty per cent of the amount already released are furnished.

Rule 7 – Contribution to National Fund:
The Central Government shall contribute to the National Fund such sum of money as considered necessary for the implementation of the Schemes.

Rule 8 – Audit:
The grants released from the National Fund to the State Governments and Union Territory Administrations shall be audited by the Comptroller and Auditor-General through respective Accountant-Generals. The internal audit shall be supervised by the office of Chief Controller of Accounts.

chunks.title: MGNREGA Audit of Schemes Rules, 2011 – Financial Audit and Social Audit
chunks.chapter: Audit of Schemes Rules 2011
chunks.text:
THE MAHATMA GANDHI NATIONAL RURAL EMPLOYMENT GUARANTEE AUDIT OF SCHEMES RULES, 2011
(Came into force on 30-6-2011)

Rule 1: For the purposes of these rules, audit of schemes shall include social audit.

Rule 2 – Audit of accounts:
(1) The audit of the accounts of a scheme for each district and of the State Employment Guarantee Fund shall be carried out by the Director, Local Fund Audit or equivalent authority or by Chartered Accountants.
(3) The accounts of the schemes of every State Government as certified with the audit report shall be forwarded to the Comptroller and Auditor General of India and the Central Government. The Central Government shall cause the audit report to be laid before each House of Parliament.
(4) The Comptroller and Auditor General of India shall have the right to conduct audit of the accounts of schemes at such intervals as he may deem fit.

Rule 3 – Social audit to be part of audit of schemes:
The State Government shall facilitate conduct of social audit of the works in every Gram Panchayat at least once in six months. A summary of findings shall be submitted to the Comptroller and Auditor General of India.

Rule 4 – Social audit facilitation:
The State Government shall identify or establish an independent Social Audit Unit to facilitate conduct of social audit by Gram Sabhas. The Social Audit Unit shall: build capacities of Gram Sabhas for conducting social audit; prepare social audit reporting formats; create awareness amongst labourers about their rights; facilitate verification of records with primary stakeholders; host social audit reports in the public domain.

Rule 5 – Social audit pre-requisites:
The Social Audit shall be a process independent of any process undertaken by the implementing agency. The implementing agency shall at no time interfere with the conduct of social audit. Resource persons deployed for facilitating social audit in a Panchayat shall not be residents of the same Panchayat.

Rule 6 – Process for conducting social audit:
The Social Audit Unit shall frame an annual calendar to conduct at least one social audit in each Gram Panchayat every six months. The resource persons shall verify: muster rolls and payments; the worksite and quantity/quality of work done; cash books, bank statements and financial records; invoices, bills, vouchers for procurement of materials.

Rule 7 – Obligations in relation to social audit:
The Programme Officer shall ensure all required information and records are provided to the Social Audit Unit at least fifteen days in advance. The District Programme Coordinator shall: ensure records are furnished; ensure corrective action is taken; take steps to recover amounts embezzled; pay misappropriated wages within seven days of recovery; initiate criminal and civil proceedings against persons who misutilised amounts. The costs of establishing the Social Audit Unit and conducting social audit shall be met by the Central Government as central assistance.

chunks.title: Wage Rate Notification – State-wise Wage Rates for Unskilled Manual Workers (Effective 1 April 2017)
chunks.chapter: Wage Rate Notification
chunks.text:
NOTIFICATION REGARDING THE WAGE RATES PAYABLE TO THE UNSKILLED MANUAL WORKERS IN DIFFERENT STATES AND UNION TERRITORIES

In exercise of the powers conferred by sub-section (1) of section 6 of the Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act, 2005, the Central Government hereby specifies the wage rate payable to unskilled manual workers, with effect from 1st April 2017:

State-wise Wage Rates (Rupees per Day):
Andhra Pradesh: Rs. 197 | Arunachal Pradesh: Rs. 177 | Assam: Rs. 183 | Bihar: Rs. 168 | Chhattisgarh: Rs. 172 | Goa: Rs. 240 | Gujarat: Rs. 192 | Haryana: Rs. 277 | Himachal Pradesh: Non-scheduled Areas Rs. 179 / Scheduled Areas Rs. 224 | Jammu and Kashmir: Rs. 179 | Jharkhand: Rs. 168 | Karnataka: Rs. 236 | Kerala: Rs. 258 | Madhya Pradesh: Rs. 172 | Maharashtra: Rs. 201 | Manipur: Rs. 204 | Meghalaya: Rs. 175 | Mizoram: Rs. 194 | Nagaland: Rs. 177 | Odisha: Rs. 176 | Punjab: Rs. 233 | Rajasthan: Rs. 192 | Sikkim: Rs. 177 | Tamil Nadu: Rs. 205 | Telangana: Rs. 197 | Tripura: Rs. 177 | Uttar Pradesh: Rs. 175 | Uttarakhand: Rs. 175 | West Bengal: Rs. 180 | Andaman and Nicobar: Andaman District Rs. 236 / Nicobar District Rs. 249 | Chandigarh: Rs. 265 | Dadra and Nagar Haveli: Rs. 218 | Daman and Diu: Rs. 195 | Lakshadweep: Rs. 237 | Puducherry: Rs. 205.
